structured_choices.data_source: EHR cohort of 10,000 breast cancer patients over five years; medical imaging subset (mammograms, MRIs) available for 5,000 patients. Data sourced from institutional cancer registry and radiology/pathology records with institutional review board approval and patient consent. Time period: 5-year historical archive with minimum 2-year follow-up for all patients in the analysis cohort.
structured_choices.label_definition: Primary outcome: maximum tumor size increase (mm) measured over a 24-month window from baseline imaging/EHR record. Baseline is defined as the initial diagnostic imaging or earliest measurable tumor size in EHR. Progression is computed as (final_size_at_24mo - baseline_size), treated as a continuous regression target. Tumor size sourced from radiology reports (imaging) and pathology/oncology notes (EHR), with standardized extraction and validation by a physician reviewer blinded to model predictions.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified temporal split by patient cohort and imaging availability. Patients are first stratified into three groups: (A) EHR-only (n=5,000), (B) EHR + imaging (n=5,000). Within each stratum, a temporal split is applied: patients enrolled/diagnosed in years 1–3 form the training set (n=6,500), years 3.5–4 form the validation set (n=2,000), and year 5 forms the test set (n=1,500). This temporal split prevents information leakage (future treatment response cannot influence baseline features) and mirrors the deployment scenario where the model predicts future progression given current baseline data. Stratification ensures both models (EHR-only baseline and multi-modal) see the same patient populations in train/val/test, enabling fair comparison and preventing the imaging-only cohort from dominating results.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (imputation, standardization, normalization, categorical encoding) are fit exclusively on the training set (years 1–3). These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. For imaging features, pixel-level normalization (intensity standardization by scanner/protocol) is fit on training imaging data only. No information from validation or test sets is used during preprocessing specification. This prevents data leakage through preprocessing statistics.
structured_choices.features: EHR features: age at diagnosis, menopausal status, BMI, smoking history, hormone receptor status (ER/PR/HER2), grade, stage at diagnosis, prior cancer history, comorbidities, medications (chemotherapy regimen, endocrine therapy, targeted therapy, start dates), and prior treatment response indicators. Imaging features (extracted from mammograms/MRIs using a pre-trained convolutional neural network backbone, ImageNet-initialized but not fine-tuned on this dataset): tumor area, tumor texture (Haralick features), margin morphology (spiculated vs. circumscribed), background parenchymal enhancement, presence of satellite lesions, lymph node involvement on imaging. Features are engineered to be temporally aligned: imaging features extracted from baseline studies, EHR features measured at or before baseline. No future treatment information or outcome-correlated features are included.
structured_choices.model: Multi-modal deep learning architecture: A shared encoder backbone ingests EHR data (structured tabular features passed through an embedding + dense layer stack), and a separate 2D CNN encoder processes normalized imaging arrays. Both encoders output fixed-size embeddings (e.g., 128 dimensions each). Embeddings are concatenated and passed through a fusion network (2–3 dense layers with batch normalization and dropout). Final output layer: linear neuron predicting continuous tumor size change (mm). Loss function: mean squared error (MSE). Architecture is trained end-to-end with early stopping on validation MSE. Hyperparameters (embedding dimensions, fusion layer widths, dropout rates, learning rate, batch size) are tuned on the validation set using random search over a pre-specified grid (50 trials, same budget as baseline).
structured_choices.baseline: EHR-only linear regression model (ridge regression with L2 regularization) trained on the same EHR feature set, evaluated on the same train/val/test splits. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength (alpha) is tuned via cross-validation on the training set (5-fold) and the best alpha is selected using validation set MSE. This baseline represents the current clinical practice (using structured EHR data) and is a fair comparison because it uses the same label, the same temporal splits, and comparable tuning investment. The comparison isolates the contribution of imaging integration to predictive performance.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Mean Squared Error (MSE) on the held-out test set, reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling (1,000 resamples) of test predictions. MSE is chosen because (1) the outcome is continuous and normally distributed, (2) MSE penalizes large prediction errors more heavily, which aligns with clinical risk: large errors in tumor progression forecasting lead to suboptimal treatment escalation decisions, (3) MSE is interpretable in clinical units (mm²), and (4) stakeholders care about prediction accuracy across all ranges of progression, not just discrimination at a threshold. Secondary analysis will report R² and Spearman correlation to contextualize performance.
secondary_metrics: Coefficient of determination (R²) on test set to measure variance explained
Mean absolute error (MAE, mm) to provide error bounds in patient-friendly units
Prediction calibration: binned calibration plot (predicted vs. observed mean progression in quintiles of predicted values) to assess whether confidence in predictions is well-founded

Subgroup performance: MSE stratified by baseline stage (early vs. advanced) and by receptor status (HR+/HER2-/triple-negative) to identify whether multi-modal gains are uniform or concentrated in specific populations
Model stability: prediction correlation between the multi-modal model and EHR-only baseline (Spearman rho) to understand whether imaging provides orthogonal information or marginal refinement
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for both baseline and multi-modal models is performed on the validation set (years 3.5–4, n=2,000). The model configuration (set of hyperparameters) that minimizes validation MSE is selected. The test set (year 5, n=1,500) is held completely separate and used only once for final evaluation reporting. No early stopping or model selection is performed on the test set. Cross-validation is not used for model selection here because the temporal structure of the data (past→future) makes k-fold CV inappropriate; temporal cross-validation could be applied as a robustness check post-hoc but is not the primary model selection mechanism.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and not accessed until all design decisions (feature engineering, preprocessing, baseline hyperparameters, multi-modal architecture choices) are finalized and validated on the validation set. Once finalized, the test set is used exactly once to compute the primary metric (MSE) and secondary metrics. No retraining, tuning, or threshold adjustment is performed after observing test results. Test results are reported with full transparency (point estimate and 95% CI, no p-values or significance claims without pre-registration).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: patient genetic predisposition and lifestyle factors (e.g., adherence to treatment, diet, exercise) influence progression rates and are not fully captured in EHR or imaging data. To control this: (1) Stratified analysis: subgroup performance is examined separately for patients receiving different treatment regimens and for those with known genetic markers (e.g., BRCA mutation status) where available in EHR. If imaging provides benefit specifically in genetically high-risk or non-adherent populations, this suggests the model is capturing additional prognostic signal. (2) Covariate adjustment: treatment regimen and adherence indicators (where available via pharmacy/visit records) are included as EHR features for both models, ensuring that if imaging captures treatment response heterogeneity, this is measured net of observed clinical factors. (3) Sensitivity analysis: the multi-modal model is re-evaluated on the subset of patients with complete genomic data to estimate performance in a more controlled population. Unknown/residual confounding (e.g., unobserved lifestyle factors) cannot be eliminated in observational data; this is acknowledged as a limitation. The experiment does not establish causality, only that multi-modal predictions outperform EHR-only predictions. (4) Population scope: analysis is restricted to patients with complete baseline imaging and EHR records; missing data patterns are documented to clarify whether results generalize to patients with incomplete records (potential selection bias). Missingness mechanisms are not assumed to be missing-at-random.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for Breast Cancer Progression Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Definition

The analysis uses the institutional cohort of 10,000 breast cancer patients with 5-year historical EHR records. A subset of 5,000 patients has accompanying baseline medical imaging (mammograms and/or MRIs) annotated with tumor characteristics. All patients must have a minimum 24-month follow-up period to compute the outcome.

The primary outcome is tumor size progression: maximum tumor size increase (mm) measured over a 24-month window from baseline. Baseline is the date of initial diagnostic imaging or earliest measurable EHR record; progression is computed as (size_at_24_months − baseline_size). Tumor measurements are extracted from radiology reports and pathology records via standardized clinical documentation, with validation by a physician reviewer blinded to model predictions.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

A stratified temporal split is used to prevent information leakage and reflect the deployment scenario. Patients are first stratified by imaging availability (EHR-only vs. EHR+imaging) to ensure both models are evaluated on matched populations. Within each stratum, a temporal split is applied:

- **Training set** (n ≈ 6,500): Patients enrolled/diagnosed in years 1–3
- **Validation set** (n ≈ 2,000): Patients enrolled/diagnosed in years 3.5–4
- **Test set** (n ≈ 1,500): Patients enrolled/diagnosed in year 5

Temporal splits are chosen because they mirror the operational deployment setting—the model receives baseline patient data and predicts future progression—and prevent the model from exploiting future treatment information. Stratification ensures both the baseline (EHR-only) and multi-modal models see the same population distribution, enabling a fair head-to-head comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**EHR Features:** Age, menopausal status, BMI, smoking history, hormone receptor status (ER/PR/HER2), tumor grade, stage, prior cancer history, relevant comorbidities, medication history (chemotherapy, endocrine therapy, targeted therapy with start dates), and prior treatment response indicators.

**Imaging Features:** Extracted from baseline mammograms/MRIs using a pre-trained CNN backbone (ImageNet initialization, not fine-tuned). Features include tumor area, texture descriptors (Haralick features), margin morphology (spiculated vs. circumscribed), background enhancement, satellite lesions, and lymph node involvement.

All preprocessing transformers (imputation, standardization, categorical encoding) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test sets. Imaging pixel normalization (intensity standardization by scanner/protocol) is fit on training images only. This prevents leakage of validation/test statistics into the preprocessing pipeline.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Multi-Modal Model:** A deep learning architecture with two parallel encoders: one processes tabular EHR features (embedding + dense layers) and one processes imaging arrays (2D CNN). Encoders output fixed-size embeddings (128 dimensions each), which are concatenated and passed through a fusion network (2–3 dense layers with batch normalization and dropout). A linear output layer predicts continuous tumor size change. Training uses MSE loss with early stopping on validation performance.

**Baseline:** Ridge regression trained on the same EHR feature set. Regularization strength (alpha) is tuned via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set and selected to minimize validation MSE. This baseline represents current clinical practice (EHR-only forecasting) and receives equivalent tuning investment (hyperparameter search over the same effort budget, e.g., 50 random trials).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Mean Squared Error (MSE) on the held-out test set, reported with 95% confidence intervals via 1,000-sample bootstrap resampling. MSE is chosen because large prediction errors have high clinical cost (incorrect treatment escalation decisions), the outcome is continuous, and MSE is interpretable in clinical units (mm²).

**Secondary Metrics:** R² (variance explained), mean absolute error (MAE, in mm), calibration plots (binned prediction vs. observed progression), and subgroup performance stratified by baseline stage and receptor status. Prediction correlation (Spearman rho) between the multi-modal model and baseline is reported to assess orthogonality of imaging information.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameters for both baseline and multi-modal models are tuned on the validation set (years 3.5–4). The model configuration minimizing validation MSE is selected. The test set is locked until all design choices are finalized and is used exactly once to compute the primary and secondary metrics. No retraining, threshold adjustment, or additional tuning is performed after observing test results.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

A known confound is patient-level heterogeneity in treatment response due to unobserved genetic factors and lifestyle choices. To address this, treatment regimen and available adherence indicators are included as EHR features in both models, ensuring fair comparison. Subgroup analyses examine whether imaging provides differential benefit for patients with known genetic markers or different treatment regimens. Sensitivity analyses are performed on the subset of patients with complete genomic data.

The experiment does not establish causality; it demonstrates that multi-modal predictions outperform EHR-only predictions in this population. Results are scoped to patients with complete baseline imaging and EHR records; missing data patterns are documented to clarify generalizability.
